statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given an array $ a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n $ , where $ n \leq 8 $ and $ a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n \leq 100 $ .

Construct a simple polygon $ ^{\text{∗}} $ with at most $ 333 $ vertices that has exactly $\frac{(a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n)!}{a_1! a_2! \cdots a_n!} $ different triangulations . It can be proven that such a polygon always exists.

A [Simple_polygon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simple_polygon) is a polygon that does not intersect itself and has no holes. In other words, no two non-consecutive edges can have common points, and consecutive edges must have exactly one common point — the vertex between them. Consecutive edges may be collinear.
A [Polygon_triangulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polygon_triangulation) of a polygon with $ m $ vertices is a set of $ m-3$ diagonals that intersect only at vertices. A diagonal is a segment between two vertices which lies inside the polygon and has exactly two common points with the polygon sides — the vertices it connects.

Input Format
The first line of each test contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 2 \leq n \leq 8 $ ) — the number of elements in the array.

The second line contains $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n $ ( $ 1 \leq a_i \leq 100 $ ) — the elements of the array.

It is guaranteed that $ a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n $ does not exceed $ 100 $ .

Output Format
In the first line, output a single integer $ m $ ( $ 3 \leq m \leq 333 $ ) — the number of vertices in the polygon.

In the $ i $ -th of the following $ m $ lines, output two integers $ x_i $ , $ y_i $ ( $ -10^6 \leq x_i, y_i \leq 10^6 $ ) — the coordinates of the $ i $ -th vertex of the polygon.

The polygon must be simple. The vertices may be given in either clockwise or counterclockwise order.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
3
1 1 2
Output
8
0 0
2 1
4 2
6 1
3 5
4 7
0 5
1 2

Sample 2
Input
2
4 1
Output
5
-2 -2
-3 1
0 3
3 1
2 -2

Note
In the first test, the required polygon has to have $ \tfrac{4!}{1! 1! 2!} = 12 $ triangulations. The following are all the triangulations of the example polygon.

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2122F/3fdba588e3b6921298eb16b4d2cf2a74d4477034.png) In the second test, the required polygon has to have $ \tfrac{5!}{4! 1!} = 5 $ triangulations.

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2122F/37e07df64c45ecffe9525467a15d7f721458acde.png)

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2122F - Colorful Polygon
We begin by solving the problem for the case $$$n = 2$$$.
Through experimentation, we can discover that a polygon consisting of two adjacent line segments with $$$a + 1$$$ and $$$b + 1$$$ points respectively has exactly $$$\binom{a + b}{b}$$$ triangulations.
Polygon with $$$\binom{6}{2} = 15$$$ triangulations.
We now generalize this construction to handle any product of binomial coefficients. The key insight is to connect these basic shapes in a manner that ensures their triangulations remain independent.
To achieve this, we extend the shorter side of the polygon to form a rectangle with additional points on two sides. This modification preserves the number of triangulations.
To connect multiple rectangles, we join them using triangular connectors, alternating the sides to which they are attached:
These connectors do not affect the total number of triangulations, as there is exactly one way to triangulate each connector.
Returning to the original problem, the most straightforward approach is to represent the required number of triangulations as a product of binomial coefficients: $$$$$$\binom{a_1 + \cdots + a_n}{a_1} \binom{a_2 + \cdots + a_n}{a_2} \cdots \binom{a_n}{a_n}.$$$$$$ However, this representation requires $$$\Theta(n \cdot s)$$$ vertices, where $$$s = a_1 + \cdots + a_n$$$, which exceeds the given constraint.
To reduce the vertex count, we employ a divide-and-conquer approach, analogous to polynomial multiplication with bounded degree sum.
We can interpret the multinomial coefficient as the number of ways to assign $$$s$$$ elements to $$$n$$$ distinct colors, where color $$$i$$$ appears exactly $$$a_i$$$ times. The construction procedure follows these steps:
First, determine the positions where the first $$$\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$$$ colors will appear.
Recursively construct representations for the first and second halves of the color set.
This divide-and-conquer strategy reduces the vertex count to $$$\Theta(s \log n)$$$, which satisfies the problem constraints.
